text: This is a sixty-three-year-old female who presented with recurrent right iliac fossa pain, colicky in nature for the last one week. No other comorbidities and no previous surgeries. Ultrasound abdomen was showing a mass colonic in origin in the right iliac fossa. Colonoscopy was showing an ulceroproliferative lesion at the IC junction. Biopsy showing moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. CEA report was CCT abdomen was showing a polypoidal mass at the IC area with irregular bowel wall thickening, probably neoplastic in origin.

So we did a, planning was a lap right radical hemicolectomy and patient position was a leg split with the surgeon standing in between the legs as well as on the right side depending upon the area of surgery. And you proceed with a five port technique where you use the camera port either in the umbilicus or in the suprapubic area and five working ports and the right side working ports both as right hand and left hand working port depending upon the infracolic and supracolic dissection.

The surgeon performs the IRETA technique, starting with retrocholic dissection to separate the mesocolon from the retroperitoneum. The avascular plane between these structures is visible, ensuring the ureter and gonadal vessels are safeguarded. The dissection progresses to mobilize the mesocolon, encountering the duodenum superiorly and medially, with careful separation of the duodenum from the mesocolon.

The surgeon exposes Gerota's fascia adjacent to the duodenum. After mobilizing the duodenum from the mesocolon, the pancreas becomes visible. The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum-pancreatic complex from the mesocolon, revealing the reflected colon.

The surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric vein with its draining vessels near the head of the pancreas. The intracolic dissection is paused, and the focus shifts to the supracolic region. The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum to access the lesser sac, carefully releasing adhesions between the pancreas and the stomach, revealing a distinct plane between the mesocolon and the stomach.

The surgeon separates the mesocolic tissues, revealing the colonic and mesocolonic vasculature. Dissection occurs between the mesocolonic vasculature and the stomach tissue, with the C-loop of the duodenum visible upon entering the correct plane. A gauze is placed to protect the underlying duodenum, connecting the infracolic and supracolic dissection planes.

The surgeon removes the final fibers of fatty tissue from the head of the pancreas, revealing the superior mesenteric vein and its draining veins. Dissection continues up to the root of the superior mesenteric vein, entering the tunnel of love. The middle colic vein is visible draining into the superior mesenteric vein, alongside the loop of end lay. The right colic vein is identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon places a gauze to safeguard the dissected area for later identification. The procedure moves to the infracolic compartment, where central vascular ligation begins. The superior mesenteric vein is clearly visible due to the patient's lack of fatty tissue. The surgeon marks the left side of the vein with a unipolar cautery and begins dissection below the ileocolic vessels. Traction is applied to the ileocolic vessels, and fibro-fatty tissue around the ileocecal root is removed, marked at least four centimeters from the root. Care is taken to avoid bleeding from small vessels during the dissection into the adventitial layer of the superior mesenteric vein, ensuring tissues to the left of the vein are part of the specimen.

The surgeon clips the ileocolic vein as it joins the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic artery is dissected up to its root, and the vessels are clipped at their origin. The dissection occurs in the retromesenteric plane, minimizing the risk of injuring surrounding structures.

The surgeon dissects on the surface of the superior mesenteric artery to identify its branches. The middle colic artery is likely identified, with a small right colic artery arising from it. The right colic artery is clipped and divided using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the metal part of the instrument is not facing down.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic vein and continues dissection around the root of the middle colic artery up to its bifurcation into the right and left branches. The right branch of the middle colic artery is visualized, and preparation is made to divide it due to the tumor located in the cecal area.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the proposed transaction area of the transverse colon and divides the lateral attachments of the ascending colon. The terminal ileum is divided at least ten centimeters away from the ileocecal valve, and the adhesions of the cecum to the abdominal wall are released. The specimen is extracted through a small midline incision near the umbilicus, followed by extracorporeal ileotransverse anastomosis.
